coredata.dc:title: Natural deep eutectic solvent-assisted preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for ratiometric determination of pirimicarb and pH
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Jianqiang
Zhao, Ning
Qu, Yuan
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: A novel green pH-sensitive fluorescent probe based on the surface passivation was designed for sensitive, rapid and reliable detection of pirimicarb (PRC). Using natural deep eutectic solvent (NADES) as the green precursor, N-CDs were hydrothermally synthesized, and combined with rhodamine B (RhB) to display strong fluorescence emission peaks at 400 and 582 nm, respectively. Upon the addition of PRC, the fluorescence sensor resulted in the enhanced fluorescence at 400 nm, while the orange signals hardly changed. Moreover, under the optimal conditions, the proposed assay exhibited super-fast, high selectivity and sensitivity for PRC with a wide linear range from 0.5 to 200 μg mL−1 and the relatively low detection limit around 0.3 μg mL−1. Importantly, real sample analyses demonstrated that the as-presented sensor had excellent potential in pesticide monitoring. Meanwhile, the enhanced photoluminescence that become emissive upon stability from N-CDs was attributed to the reduction of surface defects after the surface passivation. In virtue of superior biocompatibility, excellent optical properties and low toxicity, the N-CDs were further employed to monitor the pH value.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratometric fluorescent sensor
pH-sensitive
Natural deep eutectic solvent
Pirimicarb

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for synthesis of N-CDs
Ratiometric fluorescence assay for PRC and pH
Selectivity and anti-interference of N-CDs/RhB for PRC

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University
Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials
Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a), (b) The TEM image (inset: HRTEM) and (c), (d) the corresponding particle distribution (n = 60) of N-CDs synthesized with and without NADES, XRD pattern (e) and FT-IR spectrum of (f) of as-synthesized N-CDs, respectively.
XPS spectra of the N-CDs. (a) Full scan spectrum, high resolution spectra of C 1s (b), N 1s (c) and O 1s (d).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs and RhB; the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs (Inset: The corresponding photographs under sunlight (left), UV lamp of 254 nm). (b) The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths. (c) The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs/RhB at different pH values. (d) The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs/RhB in the presence and absence of PRC.

Effects of each factor on the intensity of N-CDs (a) the response of N-CDs synthesized with different NADES as raw materials against PRC (Before and after PRC addition). (b) molar ratio of NADES on N-CDs. (c) temperature of N-CDs (°C). (d) extraction time of N-CDs (h).

(a) The effect of pH on the N-CDs/RhB and N-CDs. (b) The ratios of N-CDs change with the reaction time. (Excitation wavelength at 325 nm) (c) Fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in various concentrations of NaCl solutions. (d) Fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at different time under 325 W irradiation.

(a) The fluorescence response of N-CDs/RhB in the presence of various concentration of PRC (0–200 μg mL−1). (b) The dynamic response ranges for PRC determination. (c) Emission spectra of N-CDs/RhB at different pH values. (d) Plot of corresponding fluorescence response (F1/F2) vs pH concentration and Linear fit (λ ex = 325 nm & λ em = 400 nm).

(a) Selectivity of as-developed ratiometric fluorescent probe towards PRC with other interferents. (b) Fluorescence intensities of N-CDs/RhB probe in the presence of various interfering chemicals (The concentration of pesticides and metals were set as 500 and 1000 μg mL−1).
Schematic illustration of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the detection of PRC and pH value.
Determination of PRC in real samples by this method (n = 3).
Analytical performance of different methods reported for PRC detection.

head.ce:title: Natural deep eutectic solvent-assisted preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for ratiometric determination of pirimicarb and pH

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel green pH-sensitive fluorescent probe based on the surface passivation was designed for sensitive, rapid and reliable detection of pirimicarb (PRC). Using natural deep eutectic solvent (NADES) as the green precursor, N-CDs were hydrothermally synthesized, and combined with rhodamine B (RhB) to display strong fluorescence emission peaks at 400 and 582 nm, respectively. Upon the addition of PRC, the fluorescence sensor resulted in the enhanced fluorescence at 400 nm, while the orange signals hardly changed. Moreover, under the optimal conditions, the proposed assay exhibited super-fast, high selectivity and sensitivity for PRC with a wide linear range from 0.5 to 200 μg mL−1 and the relatively low detection limit around 0.3 μg mL−1. Importantly, real sample analyses demonstrated that the as-presented sensor had excellent potential in pesticide monitoring. Meanwhile, the enhanced photoluminescence that become emissive upon stability from N-CDs was attributed to the reduction of surface defects after the surface passivation. In virtue of superior biocompatibility, excellent optical properties and low toxicity, the N-CDs were further employed to monitor the pH value.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, an increasing demand of agricultural products has led to the widespread use of pesticides. As we all know, the pesticides such as carbamates, which play an indispensable role in modern agriculture, have been widely used to prevent the diseases of agricultural products from pests owing to their broad biological activity, high insecticidal activity and short environmental durability [1,2]. Pirimicarb (PRC), an insecticide with moderate toxicity, is used to control all aphids except cotton aphids that are resistant to organophosphorus pesticides [3]. However, reports showed that excessive use of pesticides would inevitably cause harm to the ecological system [4]. Toxicological studies indicated that excessive use and residues of carbamate in the food chain may inhibit the activity of acetylcholinesterase in the synapse, leading to nervous system dysfunction [5,6]. Thus, highly sensitive and selective analytical methods should be established to monitor pesticides, to provide early warning and prevent their residues from increasing in the environmental matrix. Alternatively, in virtue of the potential application prospects, monitoring-pH has attracted more attentions in environmental and biomedical diagnostics [7,8]. On the one hand, the fluctuations in the pH value of body fluid are associated with many disorders. On the other hand, excessive human activities and untreated sewage can cause environmental pH changes and the acidification of soils, streams and seawater, which will further pollute our drinking water [9]. Therefore, the accurate estimation of pH value is indispensable in environmental and biomedical applications.

Up to date, several available methods, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,11] gas chromatography (GC) [12], mass spectrometry (MS) [13], spectrophotometry [14] and electrochemical methods [15,16], have been developed for the detection of PRC in different matrices. Owing to its excellent sensitivity and selectivity, GC-MS is one of the methods to identify and quantify pesticides. However, on account of thermal instability, carbamates must be derived into thermally stable derivatives prior to GC measurement [17]. Although HPLC with different detectors provides high detection accuracy, the approach rely on expensive equipment, complicated sample pretreatment procedures and need well trained operators [18]. In addition, the electrochemical analysis process is susceptible to interferences because of the similar oxidation potential and poor selectivity [19]. Generally speaking, it is still highly desired to develop an assay for PRC with high selective and sensitivity detection.

Currently, owing to outstanding features like cost-efficient, simple, high sensitivity and good selectivity, fluorescent sensors have been developed for the determination of heavy metal ions, biomolecule and pesticide residues [20]. With the developments in nanotechnology, various fluorescent materials have been widely served as fluorescent sensors, including semiconductor quantum dots, organic dyes, fluorescent proteins and lanthanide chelates [21]. In recent years, due to the unique properties of carbon dots (CDs), synthesis methods as well as the distinct precursor have attracted widespread attentions. Since the existing conventional fluorescent materials used for synthesis of CDs are usually expensive and mostly toxic, the development of simple, green and sensitive alternative material is still a worthy area of research.

Natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES), a newly emerged solvent, is usually synthesized via eutectic of hydrogen-bond acceptor (HBA) and hydrogen-bond donor (HBD) as natural materials. Notably, employing NADES as raw materials has been proposed to participate in preparing CDs, the unique nature of the strong hydrogen bond formed by HBA and HBD is expected to be a potential factor for the synthesis of various fluorophores [22]. Compared with other fluorescent nanoparticles, NADES-CDs presents unique features such as favorable biocompatibility and nontoxicity [23]. In addition, the NADES synthesized with diverse carbon sources and doped atoms (such as N, S, P) possesses different emission properties and can be nicely adapted to various measurement. Interestingly, different starting materials (HBA and HBD) and surface functionalization can considerably alter the sensing ability of CDs [24].

Notably, in most cases, the fluorescence-based probes are performed by single intensity signal, which are prone to interference from many factors during the detection process, such as the sensing material's concentration and incident light drift, which are prone to disturb in detection process. In this regard, it is not positive to determine whether fluorescent intensity change is owing to analyte-induced or environmental factors. Hence, to evade such disadvantages, dual-emission ratio fluorescent sensing platform is developed based on the ratio of the intensity at two distinct wavelengths, where one emission peak acts as a reference while the other as the response signal to recognize the analyte. Among the extensive fluorophores, rhodamine B (RhB) is widely used as a fluorescent probe due to its various excellent properties, including outstanding water solubility, high fluorescence quantum yield and photostability [25]. Recently, in virtue of the outstanding properties for the detection of analytes, rhodamine-based fluorescence sensors have been reported as a platform for the development of novel fluorophores [26,27].

In this paper, nitrogen - doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with strong blue fluorescence was prepared by hydrothermal method using NADES synthesized from dl-malic acid and glycerol as green precursor and urea as nitrogen source, which could be used for pH value and PRC detection (Scheme 1 ). The as-prepared N-CDs displayed excellent water stability, low toxicity, and photochemical stability with abundant functional groups, which was highly desirable to construct ratio fluorescence sensor. Subsequently, enhanced-N-CDs-RhB system was designed as a ratio fluorescence probe for the determination of PRC with extremely fast response time, excellent sensitivity and selectivity. Besides, the feasibility of this method was further verified as a novel pH-sensitive probe.

dl-malic acid (DL-MA) (C4H6O5), citric acid (CA) (C6H8O7), l-lysine (C6H14N2O2), l-methionine (C5H11NO2S), l-arginine (C6H14N4O2) and rhodamine B (RhB) were purchased from Bide Pharmatech Ltd (Shanghai, China). Glycerin (C3H8O3) and urea (CH4N2O) were supplied by Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). Pesticides such as PRC, carbendazim were all procured from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). The interfering test metal ion reagents were supplied by Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). All reagents used in this work were analytical grade and used directly.

The morphology of N-CDs synthesized with and without NADES were analyzed using a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an Escalab250Xi (Thermo Fisher, USA), and the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was obtained from D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker). Zeta potential measurements of N-CDs were collected by a Malvern Zetasizer (Nano-ZS90, USA). A Nicolet IS50 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) was applied to measure FT-IR and research functional group information of N-CDs. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on UV-5500PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Metash Instruments, China). All the fluorescence spectra were collected on a F97 (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd. China).

NADES was prepared by using DL-MA as the HBA as well as glycerin for HBD. DL-MA and glycerin were added into a 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask at different molar ratios (DL-MA: glycerin, 1:1, 1:3, 1:6, and 1:9). The mixtures were then mixed and heated with stirring at 60 °C until the uniform and transparent liquid phase appeared. The NADES was then left at room temperature for later use. All NADES synthesis methods were similar.

N-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. In brief, 0.7 g of NADES and 0.3 g of urea were mixed and dissolved in 20 mL deionized water with ultrasonic until the mixture was completely dissolved. Then, the mixed solution was allowed to undergo further reaction in a Teflon-lined autoclave and incubated at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, a yellow-brown liquid product was obtained. Afterward, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min, followed by a 0.22 μm filter membrane to separate the large particles. Finally, the N-CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further experiment.

In the development process of new luminescent materials, the luminous efficiency of the materials was very important. Compared with the relative quantum yield, the determination of absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (Abs-QY) was more stable, accurate and can reduce experimental system errors [28].

The Abs-QY was defined as Eq:
Abs−QY = L sample ÷ (E reference − E sample)
where Lsample was the emission intensity, and Ereference and Esample were the intensities of the excitation light that was not absorbed by the reference sample and the sample, respectively [29].

For the detection of PRC, 50 μL of N-CDs (50 μg mL−1), 200 μL of RhB (80 μg mL−1) reagent were first injected into a centrifuge tube with 2.8 mL phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7) and shaken thoroughly. After mixing, 80 μL of various concentrations (0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200 μg mL−1) of PRC were mixed with the above solution in centrifuge tube, respectively. Fluorescence spectra were recorded after the mixture was vortexed for 50 s at room temperature.

The analytical process of prepared fluorescence sensor was evaluated by recording the fluorescent signal ratio (F1/F2) of N-CDs (F1) and RhB (F2). The selectivity of the sensing system with N-CDs/RhB probe was achieved by adding various interfering cations and pesticides to the fluorescent probe at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The emission slit width was set as 10 nm and all experimental conditions were consistent during the measurement.

To explore the fluorescence response of N-CDs in phosphate buffer solutions of different pH (pH = 2.0–12.0), 80 μL (50 μg mL−1) and 200 μL rhodamine B solution (80 μg mL−1) were added to the phosphate buffer, respectively. Shaking acutely at room temperature and recording the fluorescence spectra of each solution with the fluorescence signal ratio (F1/F2).

To evaluate the potential of as-prepared ratiometric fluorescence probe and the feasibility of this assay in the practical application of PRC, samples of tea, apple, and cucumber sold in the local market were determined.

0.1 g of tea was soaked in 10 mL of ultrapure water at 80 °C for 20 min, followed by the addition of different concentrations of PRC. The vegetable and fruit puree crushed by the juice extractor, were mixed with 10 mL methanol in a 50 mL centrifuge tube which were added by a series of concentrations of PRC. After that, the resulting solutions were ultrasound at 40 W for 2 min to fully dissolve the samples in the system. Further, to distribute in the real samples, the mixed 4 g of anhydrous magnesium sulfate and 1 g of sodium chloride were added to the test tube, vortexed for 1 min, and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min to collect the supernatant. In addition, the transferred supernatant was heated via water bath at 40 °C, and dried with a nitrogen stream until almost dry. Finally, the mixture was redissolved with 80 μL methanol.

The morphology and size of the N-CDs synthesized with and without NADES were firstly characterized by TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 1 (a) and (b), both N-CDs synthesized with and without NADES were all spherical in shape and well-dispersed in aqueous solution. Their size distributions of two types of N-CDs were estimated to be 4.2–6.8 nm and 2.1–5 nm, with the average size of 5.7 and 3.7 nm, respectively (n = 60) (Fig. 1(c and d)). In addition, the high-resolution TEM images (inset in Fig. 1 (a, b)) indicated that the clear crystalline structure of parallel crystal planes of these N-CDs were observed with a carbon lattice spacing of 0.22 nm and 0.24 nm, which resembled the (100) graphite facet of graphite carbon [30]. Notably, compare of the two types of carbon dots, N-CDs synthesized with NADES showed a larger average size (~5.7 nm) due to the formation of many hydrogen bonds [31]. Moreover, to confirm its crystalline nature, the XRD pattern of N-CDs synthesized with NADES was presented in Fig. 1 (e) and exhibited a distinct broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 24.6°, which was attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms [32,33]. In addition, due to the hybrid nature of N-CDs, such as the (100) graphite surfaces similar to graphite carbon, it indicated the graphite structure of carbon atoms [34].

The FT-IR spectrum of the surface group of N-CDs was shown in Fig. 1 (f). According to the measurement, a typical peak at 3459 cm−1 could be seen, which was contributed to O–H and N–H vibrations [35]. Furthermore, in the characteristic peak of carboxylic acid, the peak shape was wide and blunt. In addition, the CH stretching vibration was easily submerged by the hydroxyl peak contained in the carboxylic acid. As shown in Fig. 1(a), the short peak of the unsaturated hydrocarbon stretching vibration peak at 2849 cm− 1 also confirmed the presence of carboxyl groups. The presence of absorption around 1644 cm−1 resulted a sharp peak, which indicated that there were nitrogenous groups on N-CDs, relating to the HN–CO. Two peaks at 1399 and 1112 cm−1 were associated with in-plane bending vibration of O–H and asymmetric stretching vibration of C–N [36], respectively. In addition, the broad absorption around 682 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum was attributed to N–H, which also showed that the N-doped nature on N-CDs.

Thereafter, the elemental composition information of N-CDs was tested by XPS analyses (Fig. 2 ). As showed in Fig. 2 (a), the full scan XPS spectrum included three distinct binding energy states, where the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s signals were located at 533, 401, and 285 eV, separately. The fitted peaks of C 1s revealed the four main peaks centered at 284.5, 284.7, 286.4, 288.5 eV, which were respectively ascribed to C–N, C–H, CO and HN–CO bonds (Fig. 2 (b)) [37]. As plotted in Fig. 2 (c), for the binding energy state of N-CDs, the high-resolution spectrum of N 1s was divided into three deconvoluted peaks at 400.3, 401.6 and 406.7 eV, belonging to the C–N, N–H and NO bonds, respectively [8]. The peaks at 531.5, 532.3 and 532.6 eV of O 1s (Fig. 1 (d)) were attributed to O–H, CO, and HO–CO bonds [38]. The XPS dates confirmed the presence of –COOH and –NH2 functional groups in the surface of N-CDs.

To explore their optical properties, the optical information of N-CDs and RhB were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 (a), there was an obvious absorption peak around 249 nm in N-CDs spectrum, corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC [39]. The as-prepared N-CDs presented an obvious excitation peak at about 325 nm, and the intense blue emission peak was located at 400 nm. Meanwhile, the emission wavelength of N-CDs was basically unchanged with various excitation wavelengths ranging from 230 to 320 nm, which indicated that N-CDs had λex-independent feature (Fig. 3 (b)). And the N-CDs showed the quantum yield up to 8.74%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for synthesis of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of NADES type, molar ratio, temperature and time on the synthesis of N-CDs were firstly probed. As plotted in Fig. 4 (a), among a variety of NADES synthesized based on amino acids and glycerol, the fluorescent probe synthesized by DL-MA and glycerol had a great response against PRC. In order to elucidate the role of NADES, N-CDs were directly synthesized using DL-MA and glycerol as raw materials without intermediate generation of NADES. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity was not satisfactory with the identical proportion of raw materials. This might be due to the fact that hydrogen bonds were not formed. The fluorescence intensity increased significantly when the molar ratio was changed from 1:1 to 1:3 of DL-MA to glycerol and showed no further change thereafter (Fig. 4 (b)).

When the molar amount of glycerin was more than that of DL-MA, the products showed higher fluorescence intensity. It was well known that the formation of NADES would generate a large number of hydrogen bonds. According to TEM (Fig. 1 (a)), due to the formation of hydrogen bonds, N-CDs synthesized from NADES had a relatively large size and particle size distribution. In virtue of reversibility, specificity, adjustability and relatively high strength of noncovalent interactions, hydrogen bonds mediating has been proved to be a promising strategy for controlling CDs synthesis [25]. But with more than 1:3 ratio of DL-MA to glycerin, the products showed lower intensity (Fig, 4 (b)), which may be the effect of more glycerol forming intramolecular hydrogen bonds [40,41]. The increase of the overall net repulsive interaction was indicated by logarithmic viscosity and positive molar excess, which interfered with the balance between hydrogen bond donor and hydrogen bond acceptor of NADES via intricate interplay of interactions within the system [40–42], thus indirectly affected the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs. After that, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs along with different temperatures (120–200 °C) and time (2–8 h) were shown in Fig. 4 (c) and (d). This increasing trend of fluorescence intensity with increasing temperature was related to the physical properties of NADES. It was clearly showed that the fluorescence intensity increased from 2 to 6 h and then reached the maximum at 8 h. However, no significant increase was found when the synthesis time increased up to 8 h. Based on the cost of the reaction equipment and other factors, the 6 h synthesis time was selected. Hence, the optimized conditions for the synthesis of the N-CDs were as: molar ratio of NADES components (1:3); temperature of 200 °C and the synthesis time of 6 h. These optimized values were saved in subsequent experiments.

In this work, N-CDs were synthesized with a mass ratio of NADES: Urea 3:1 as the raw material and used as the basis for follow-up research. In order to obtain an excellent analytical performance, N-CDs/RhB was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity ratio (F1/F2), where the F1 and F2 were related to the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and RhB. The optimal fluorescence intensity ratio (F1/F2) was obtained by optimizing pH value and reaction time.

The pH value of the sensing medium was essential to analyze the detection performance of the probe. Fig. 5 (a) showed that the prepared ratio fluorescent sensor had pH-dependent luminescence property. After introducing of PRC, the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system showed a growth trend in the pH ranges of 2–6 and 8–10, and hardly changed when the pH value varied from 6 to 7. Hence, pH = 7 was selected as the optimal condition for the analytical detection. The fluorescence signal of N-CDs remained stable with pH from 2 to 7. When the pH > 8, the fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced, which could be attributed to the deprotonation process of the carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs. Based on the high sensitivity of fluorescent probes to alkalis, a supplementary experiment for pH testing was designed.

Fig. 5 (b) showed the optimization process of the incubation time, which was one of the key conditions for the rapid sensing procedure. After adding PRC, a sharp increase of F1/F2 value could be observed with the extension of incubation time in the first 40 s, indicating the N-CDs was ready for work. The fluorescence intensity remained stable after the addition of PRC for 50 s, so in the next measurement, 50 s was selected as the incubation time.

To evaluate the stability of N-CDs, the sensing system was mixed in salt solutions of different concentrations (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1 mol L−1) and the fluorescence intensities were recorded. Fig. 5 (c) showed that the N-CDs were relatively stable. As displayed in Fig. 5 (d), the N-CDs had great photobleaching resistance, the fluorescence intensity showed a slow downward trend within 70 min and retained about 90% under continuous radiation by UV light at 325 nm for 120 min.

The ratiometric fluorescence assay of PRC has been evaluated by recording the fluorescence signal of N-CDs/RhB nanohybrid. Upon excitation at 325 nm, the N-CDs and RhB exhibited obvious fluorescence signals at 400 and 582 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs gradually increased with the addition of different PRC concentrations, while the fluorescence signal of RhB only slightly changed (Fig, 6 (a)). PRC contained diazine structure, was a pyrimidine carbamate which belonged to broad spectrum pesticide. Diazine, also known as o-diazobenzene, was an organic compound with the chemical formula C4H4N2. Due to its structural characteristic, PRC will produce weak fluorescence. Noteworthily, in this study, NADES synthesized N-CDs produced a strong fluorescence emission peak at 400 nm under excitation at 325 nm, in the presence of N-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of PRC at 400 nm was enhanced significantly (Fig. 3 (d)). Therefore, the detection of low concentrations of PRC can be achieved, which was helpful for the accurate quantification of PRC. This was ascribed to the sensitization effect of N-CDs on PRC according the literature [43,44]. It was speculated that there was a valence bond between the functional groups on the surface of N-CDs and the PRC, which will reduce the surface defects of the N-CDs and increase the fluorescence intensity.

From the perspective of N-CDs, the strong fluorescence emission of N-CDs also mainly came from the surface-doped heteroatom [45]. In other words, the FT-IR and XPS consistently indicated that these functional groups, such as –COOH, –NH2 and –OH, were successfully modified on the surface of the N-CDs. These functional groups improved the hydrophilicity and stability of N-CDs in aqueous systems for further functionalization and surface passivation [46]. The urea played two key roles in the formation of N-CDs, as the precursor for N-doping providing high quantum yield [45] and the passivation agent enhancing fluorescence intensity of N-CDs [47].

In addition, impressed by optical merits and wide surface functionalities of N-CDs, a method was established to explore alkaline protons recognition capability. The feasibility of the designed probe for pH value detection was further studied. Fig. 3 (c) represented the photoluminescence spectrum of as-developed N-CDs/RhB varying with pH values. Besides, XPS results displayed that urea formed amide bonds during the preparation of N-CDs, which may explain the stability of fluorescence intensity of N-CDs under acidic conditions (Fig. 5 (a)). With increasing pH values, the emission intensity increased significantly at 400 nm, and reached to a maximum value at pH = 11.2, accompanying an obvious blue shift in the emission wavelength from 416 to 400 nm. Interestingly, the emission wavelength of RhB only underwent a slight blue shift, and emission intensity remained basically unchanged from the acid region to the alkali region. Especially, the theory of fluorescent enhancement from structural defects have been widely accepted. As shown in Fig, 3 (c), the low fluorescence intensity under acidic conditions and the emission peak blue shift might be originated from surface defects of N-CDs. In view of the small size of CDs, the wave functions of electrons and holes cover the entire CDs and its surface groups, hence the excited electrons would exist on the interface between the solution and the surface of the CDs before returning to the ground state [48,49]. On the other hand, the fluorescent intensity was affected by the two processes of radiative transition and non-radiative transition. Unlike the acidic environment, the interaction between the surface and the solution molecules was weak in the alkaline environment. In other words, the non-radiative transition process was more intense in alkaline environment [24]. As the pH increased, under alkaline conditions, reduction of surface defects could lead to a significant increase in emission intensity at 400 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence assay for PRC and pH
ce:section.ce:para: The as-developed N-CDs/RhB of the sensing system was evaluated for the quantitative detection of PRC after the optimized experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs increased at 400 nm, and the signal intensity of RhB remained basically unchanged near 528 nm in various concentrations of PRC. As displayed in Fig. 6 (b), the linear dynamic response was depicted as F1/F2 = 0.0057 [PRC] + 0.2303. In addition, the low detection limit was calculated to be 0.3 μg mL−1, according to 3σ/slope (σ was the standard deviation of blank samples) [46]. The fluorescence spectrum of pH values was plotted in Fig. 6 (c). With the increase of pH values, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually increased. As shown in Fig. 6 (d), similar to PRC measurement method, a linear curve of the N-CDs fluorescence intensity against pH from 8.3 to 11.2 was observed (y = 0.2851 [pH] - 2.102, R2 = 0.995). These results indicated that the as-developed N-CDs/RhB sensor performed high sensitivity to alkaline region and wide dynamic range to PRC. Therefore, the as-prepared N-CDs/RhB ratio fluorescence probe could be served as a candidate for the PRC detection and applied as a novel kind of nanoprobes for pH value sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of N-CDs/RhB for PRC
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity investigation was one of the vital analytical evaluation indicators, which was necessary for fluorescent sensor. Hence, the selectivity of N-CDs/RhB system was investigated and given in Fig. 7 (a). Various common existing interfering cations and pesticides were tested, such as Cu2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, imidacloprid, acetamiprid, thiacloprid, paclobutrazol, carbendazim, trichlorfon, napropamide, chlorpyrifos, fipronil, methomyl, metalaxyl, myclobutanil, carbaryl and metolcarb. It was clear that only PRC could enhance the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 7 (b), the addition of interfering chemicals displayed negligible impact on the ratio of F1/F2. Thus, in this experiment, as-synthesized fluorescence probe was highly selective towards PRC.

The applicability of the as-prepared N-CDs/RhB was evaluated by detecting PRC in the real samples (tea, apples and cucumbers). In this test, the real samples were spiked with the known concentrations for PRC and then the fluorescence intensities were recorded, respectively. The recoveries of actual samples were obtained in this experiment ranging from 95% to 104%, with RSD between 0.94%–7.92%. The results listed in Table 1 demonstrated that this method could be used in actual samples detection.

In summary, we have developed a novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on N-CDs and RhB for the determination of PRC and pH values. Meanwhile, the as-prepared N-CDs exhibited excellent water solubility, photostability, green and environmental friendliness. In comparison with the proposed detection methods for PRC in Table 2 [11–13,15,16,43], N-CDs/RhB indicated excellent analytical performances including rapid response, high sensitivity and selectivity. Furthermore, as-prepared ratio fluorescent probe was successfully applied to detect PRC ranging from 0.5 to 200 μg mL−1, with a low detection limit as 0.3 μg mL−1. More interestingly, N-CDs showed pH-sensitive behavior feature. Based on the surface effect, the strongly pH-dependent photoluminescence emission indicated the presence of abundant of acidic or basic functional groups on the surfaces of N-CDs. Affected by the transition efficiency of stimulated emission, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs increases significantly under alkaline conditions, following the fluorescence intensity displayed a linear relationship against pH values from 8.3 to 11.2. Consequently, the proposed strategy demonstrated that N-CDs/RhB was a promising candidate for ratiometric sensing.

Jianqiang Song: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ning Zhao: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Yuan Qu: Writing – review & editing. Longshan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).
